content: read reviews/465.md. if the summary section of the checklist review indicates the review passed, do nothing. if it failed, make relevant updates that are needed to deepmind/465.lean accordingly. if in doubt, reference CHECKLIST.md, which was the source document used when a previous instance of claude produced reviews/465.md.

    35→
    36→## 6. Theorem Statements
    37→
    38→- [x] Both declarations use the `theorem` keyword.
    39→- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_465` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
    40→- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib conventions: `erdos_465`, `erdos_465.variants.strong`.
    41→- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research solved, AMS 11 52]`.
    42→- [x] Both theorems have docstrings.
    43→- [x] Docstrings reference source papers: weak form references `[Sa76]`, strong form references `[Ko01]`.
    44→
    45→### `answer()` usage
    46→
    47→The problem states bounds on N(X, δ) as direct assertions (proved results). Both theorems are stated as **Case 3 — direct assertions** with no `answer()` wrapper, which is correct:
    48→
    49→- [x] The weak form asserts N(X, δ) = o(X) directly as a universal statement (∀ δ, ε, ∃ X₀, ...).
    50→- [x] The strong form asserts N(X, δ) ≪ X^{1/2} directly (∃ C, ∀ X, ...).
    51→- [x] No `answer()` needed since neither theorem asks a yes/no question or requests a specific value — both state proved bounds.
    52→
    53→## 7. Category Attribute
    54→
    55→- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[category ...]`.
    56→- [x] Category value is `research solved` — both results are mathematically proved (Sárközy 1978, Konyagin 2001) but not formally verified in Lean.
    57→- [x] Not marked `research open`, so the `sorry`-free check does not apply; `sorry` in proof bodies is expected for `research solved` (formally unverified).
    58→
    59→## 8. AMS Attribute
    60→
    61→- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
    62→- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `11 52`.
    63→- [x] Numbers in ascending numeric order: 11 < 52. ✓
    64→- [x] No duplicate numbers.
    65→- [x] Both `11` (Number theory) and `52` (Convex and discrete geometry) are valid AMS MSC2020 codes.
    66→

    70→- [x] No `sorry` in `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations.

    73→

    77→- [x] Variant lives in the same file as the main theorem.

    79→- [x] Variant has a docstring documenting that it is the stronger Konyagin bound (X^{1/2} vs o(X)).
    80→

    83→- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
    84→- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `ℝ`, `∀`, `∈`, `≥`, `≤`, `≠`, `·`.
    85→- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
    86→- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.

Both the main theorem (weak form, Sárközy) and the variant (strong form, Konyagin) are correctly formalized as direct assertions with `research solved` category. file.content:
# Review: `deepmind/465.lean` — Erdős Problem 465

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports` at line 17.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (lines 19–32).
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 465`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link: `*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/465](https://www.erdosproblems.com/465)`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (defines N(X, δ) as the maximum packing number in a disk with distance-to-integer constraints).
- [x] Bibliography entries for related papers: `[Sa76]` (Sárközy 1978) and `[Ko01]` (Konyagin 2001).

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos465 ... end Erdos465`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos465`.
- [x] No `open` / `open scoped` statements needed; none present. N/A.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] `distNearestInt465` has a docstring: `/-- The distance of a real number from the nearest integer. -/`.
- [x] Marked `noncomputable` (uses `Int.fract`).
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
- [x] Function returning a value uses `lowerCamelCase`: `distNearestInt465`.
- [x] No problem-specific notation needed; `local notation` not used. N/A.
- [ ] No `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks for the custom definition. *Optional but recommended — could add a test verifying e.g. `distNearestInt465 0.3 = 0.3` and `distNearestInt465 0.7 = 0.3`.*

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Both declarations use the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_465` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib conventions: `erdos_465`, `erdos_465.variants.strong`.
- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research solved, AMS 11 52]`.
- [x] Both theorems have docstrings.
- [x] Docstrings reference source papers: weak form references `[Sa76]`, strong form references `[Ko01]`.

### `answer()` usage

The problem states bounds on N(X, δ) as direct assertions (proved results). Both theorems are stated as **Case 3 — direct assertions** with no `answer()` wrapper, which is correct:

- [x] The weak form asserts N(X, δ) = o(X) directly as a universal statement (∀ δ, ε, ∃ X₀, ...).
- [x] The strong form asserts N(X, δ) ≪ X^{1/2} directly (∃ C, ∀ X, ...).
- [x] No `answer()` needed since neither theorem asks a yes/no question or requests a specific value — both state proved bounds.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[category ...]`.
- [x] Category value is `research solved` — both results are mathematically proved (Sárközy 1978, Konyagin 2001) but not formally verified in Lean.
- [x] Not marked `research open`, so the `sorry`-free check does not apply; `sorry` in proof bodies is expected for `research solved` (formally unverified).

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `11 52`.
- [x] Numbers in ascending numeric order: 11 < 52. ✓
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] Both `11` (Number theory) and `52` (Convex and discrete geometry) are valid AMS MSC2020 codes.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Both theorems use the multi-line `by\n  sorry` format, which is acceptable.
- [x] No `sorry` in `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations.
- [x] No informal proof sketches (both are `research solved`, not `research open`).
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant named `erdos_465.variants.strong` — follows `erdos_N.variants.<descriptor>` convention.
- [x] Variant lives in the same file as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research solved, AMS 11 52]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring documenting that it is the stronger Konyagin bound (X^{1/2} vs o(X)).

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `ℝ`, `∀`, `∈`, `≥`, `≤`, `≠`, `·`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` not run in this review. No obvious issues expected; standard Mathlib types (`EuclideanSpace`, `Finset`, `dist`, `Real.sqrt`, `Int.fract`) are used correctly.
- [ ] Custom linter compliance not verified by execution. File structure conforms to all linter-checked patterns (copyright, category, AMS, answer, namespace).

---

## Summary

**Status: PASS**

The file is well-structured and conforms to all checklist requirements. Both the main theorem (weak form, Sárközy) and the variant (strong form, Konyagin) are correctly formalized as direct assertions with `research solved` category. The custom definition `distNearestInt465` is properly docstringed and marked `noncomputable`. AMS codes (11, 52) are appropriate for this number-theoretic / discrete geometry problem.

**Minor suggestion (non-blocking):** Consider adding a `@[category test]` example to sanity-check `distNearestInt465` on a concrete input.

content.text: The review status is **PASS**. No changes needed.
